metadata.title: String of Pearls — China's Indian-Ocean port/base network, the framing dispute, and the Hambantota 'debt-trap' correction (both sides, dated)
metadata.status: the ports/base + dates + the Hambantota lease are FACT (port operators, govt announcements, Chatham House/Brautigam); 'String of Pearls' is an analytical FRAMING (US/India), not a Chinese program — flagged; the 'debt-trap seizure' reading of Hambantota is CORRECTED per the scholarship
metadata.frame: Maritime extension of the corridors block: the network of Chinese-built/operated ports + a base ringing the Indian Ocean. KEY DISCIPLINE: 'String of Pearls' is a US/India strategic-analysis term (Booz Allen, 2005) for an ENCIRCLEMENT reading; China frames the same ports as commercial (the Maritime Silk Road / BRI). Both framings recorded; intent not imputed. The Hambantota 99-year lease (2017) — the canonical 'debt-trap' example — is CORRECTED: scholarship (Chatham House, Deborah Brautigam) finds Sri Lanka's crisis was driven mostly by WESTERN capital-market bonds, not Chinese loans, and the lease raised FX rather than being a loan-default seizure. India's counter ('Necklace of Diamonds') included. Attaches to China, India, BRI, COSCO, Infrastructure_Corridors.

the_pearls: The nodes (fact): GWADAR (Pakistan) — the CPEC flagship deep-water port on the Arabian Sea; HAMBANTOTA (Sri Lanka) — leased 99 years to China Merchants Port (2017); the DJIBOUTI base — China's FIRST overseas military base (opened 2017), beside the US's Camp Lemonnier; KYAUKPYU (Myanmar) — deep-sea port + oil/gas pipelines giving China an Indian-Ocean outlet bypassing the Malacca Strait; PIRAEUS (Greece) — COSCO's majority-owned Mediterranean gateway into Europe. Others frequently cited: Chittagong (Bangladesh), the Maldives, Port Sudan. grade: fact (each port/lease/base is documented).
the_framing: The dispute is over INTERPRETATION, recorded both ways (no imputed unitary intent): (A) ENCIRCLEMENT reading (US/India analysts) — a 'String of Pearls' of dual-use ports that could become naval footholds around India, evidenced by the Djibouti base + PLA-Navy port calls. (B) COMMERCIAL reading (China) — these are Maritime-Silk-Road trade/energy logistics (esp. the Malacca-bypass via Gwadar/Kyaukpyu pipelines), not bases. The honest middle: most are commercial ports with latent dual-use potential; only Djibouti is an actual base to date. grade: both documented; 'naval-base network' is contested beyond Djibouti.
the_hambantota_correction: The canonical 'debt-trap' case, corrected per the scholarship: the story that China 'seized' Hambantota when Sri Lanka couldn't repay a loan is NOT what the record shows. (1) Sri Lanka's 2017 debt distress was driven mainly by WESTERN capital-market sovereign bonds (ISBs) + a strong dollar, not Chinese lending (Chinese debt was a minority share). (2) The port was NEVER loan collateral; Sri Lanka LEASED it (99 yrs, ~$1.1B) to China Merchants to RAISE foreign exchange to pay OTHER (mostly non-Chinese) debts. (3) Chatham House + Deborah Brautigam (the 'Rise of the (red) tape' / debt-trap research) conclude the 'debt-trap diplomacy' framing is largely a myth here. grade: 'China seized the port for unpaid debt' is UNSUPPORTED/corrected; the lease itself is fact.
the_india_counter: India's response (fact, framing-symmetric): a 'Necklace of Diamonds' strategy — securing access at Chabahar (Iran, also an INSTC node), Sittwe (Myanmar), Duqm (Oman), Sabang (Indonesia), Assumption Island (Seychelles), plus Quad/AUKUS naval cooperation — to counter perceived encirclement. Like 'String of Pearls,' 'Necklace of Diamonds' is an analytical label, not an official program. grade: fact (the access agreements); the label is analytical.
the_honest_reading: The physical facts are clear: China built/operates a ring of Indian-Ocean ports (Gwadar, Hambantota, Kyaukpyu, Piraeus) and one military base (Djibouti). What is contested — and recorded both ways — is whether this is an ENCIRCLEMENT network ('String of Pearls,' a US/India framing) or COMMERCIAL Maritime-Silk-Road logistics (China's framing); beyond Djibouti, the 'naval base' reading is not established. And the case most cited as proof of predatory intent — Hambantota — is corrected by the scholarship: it was an FX-raising lease amid a mostly-Western-bond debt crisis, not a loan-default seizure. India's 'Necklace of Diamonds' mirrors the dynamic. Intent unattributed; framings flagged; the debt-trap myth corrected. Overlay; excluded from the proofs.
